## **Prologue** (20 June 2026)  
I love reading design diaries and thought it would be appropriate to publish my thoughts from working on Gizmos into the public Git repository. Hopefully someone finds it interesting. I strongly considered running this through AI to scrub personal info or irrelevant details, but I think that would detract from the value. I will be redacting some info, but my goal is to publish it with as little changes as possible. Giving it another read is fun and nostalgic for me. Hopefully someone finds it interesting or fun to read.

I also feel compelled to point out I am not necessarily proud of how significant portions of this codebase were written. I cut corners and used sloppy hacks and workarounds because either I didn’t know better or got lazy. I can rationalize some of these decisions in that it’s a one-off game for BGA, not an enterprise grade application, but there is something to be said for doing things well for its own sake. I don’t think I had that mindset at all while writing Gizmos. I’m not sure that I really do now either, but I’ve at least accepted that I care what other people think. So this is to the people who read my code and think it’s shit. Yeah maybe it is, but I am proud of the end result. Gizmos was wildly popular on BGA release and has continued to see substantial play. Phil Walker Harding and his team deserve the vast majority of that credit, but I do think I made some good decisions about the UI design which absolutely matter. I am comfortably proud of that.

That was all before re-reading and now I have finished. Surprisingly very little to redact. Some dead links. I was certainly naive but it's fun to see how much I've learned. Things I found interesting or was proud of would no longer meet that threshold today. I think that's ultimately good. Crazy how long I went down a bad design for Converters before finally scrapping and overhauling. A good lesson to learn there. I have had a tendancy to overengineer things that should actually be quite simple. End is quite abrupt. What happened between Oct and Feb and why didn't I write any entries? Oh well. Enjoyed the read.

## **10 July 2022**

I wish I had started this diary much earlier.  I started working on Gizmos on June 14th and have logged well over 40 hours at this point.  There have been many setbacks along the way and I'll try to recall them for posterity and maybe to help someone else have an easier time at BGA development.  I'll start from the beginning.

I played on boardgamearena.com for the first time with friends during covid some time in 2021\.  After my second time playing, I did a deeper dive into the games and realized it has a competitive ladder which is a major hook for me.  I started with Railroad Ink but quickly got into 7 Wonders Duels followed by Splendor, Little Factory, and the Abyss reaching top 100 in all except 7 Wonders Duels.

I'm a software engineer and I've long sought branching my profession out into my hobbies.  BGA, an opensource platform for building and playing games, seems like a great place to start.  But to be sufficiently motivated I needed to pick a game I actually like at least while I'm learning the development framework, a significant undertaking.  My first choice would have been Everdell, but apparently the publisher has already been contacted and rejected adaptation by BGA.  Alas, my second choice, Gizmos, was still a very good one and turns out the publisher, CMON, has already agreed to have one of their other games built on BGA which was very promising.  I began work before getting the green light since in the worst case scenario I could at least learn a lot.

Like most frameworks, getting started was pretty daunting.  The documentation is reasonably friendly but dense.  I won't cover it in detail as that's what the documentation is for, but I'll try to give a surface-level overview of any of the pieces that are relevant as they come up.  Depending on your background with BGA and software development I will likely either be way overexplaining or way underexplaining.  Hopefully I hit the middleground right for someone.

I started with a very dumbed down version of the UI including the 6 energies in the row (randomly generated every time the page reloads), 2 level 3 cards, 3 level 2 cards, and 4 level 1 cards matching what is seen on the Gizmos board.  I added the deck next to the cards a little bit later.  I made each element clickable and stubbed out a JS function showing an info popup saying something like "TODO: implement onEnergySelect".  The assignment of onclicks to elements in the UI is facilitated via a framework called "dojo" which I had never used but apparently is fairly ubiquitous for things like this.

I guess I should back-up a bit and say that the user interface is built on HTML/CSS/JS but has an added option to iteratively build out chunks of the UI using PHP.  At first I thought this might be helpful, but it turns out it's much less flexible than using JS instead.  It's possible I'm simply missing something but with my current knowledge I would not recommend using the X.view.php file in any capacity.  Everything you can do there can be done better in your X.js file and in my opinion is much easier to write and maintain though there's a heavy dose of personal preference there as I have written way more JS than PHP.

I took a brief detour to work on the player cards as well.  If you've played on BGA you know that in any game each player has a "card" or "box" listing out some basic information relevant to the game including their current score.  Naturally it made sense to me to list out the number of energies each player has.  This was pretty easy to knock out in the matter of an hour or so.  Just some calls to dojo to add new div/span elements inside each player card with a colored energy icon next to each.

One more tangent: BGA has been my introduction to spritesheets.  The principle behind spritesheets is that HTTP requests for images are expensive.  I.e. each time someone loads your game, their browser has to go retrieve all of the various images that go along with it.  Because of how HTTP was built, retrieving 1 large image is substantially faster than many smaller images even if the sum of their sizes is some significant threshold smaller.  Thus to maximize efficiency, we put all of the images together into one large image and use CSS attributes like background-size to show only the small portion of that image.  The user doesn't even know it's happening unless they open their dev-console.  Pretty cool, but a bit of a pain to setup for 108 unique cards \- I'll get to that later…

Next is the state machine.  I may have actually started on the state machine before the UI, but I don't remember anymore.  This is another interesting part of the BGA framework.  At any given time the game is in one of a dev-defined list of states which is stored in a built-in database table.  The current state defines which player(s) is/are active and what the valid following states are.  Any player state indicates that the game is waiting for a player to perform an action.  The game JS includes hooks to show specific action buttons based on the player state.  A game state hooks into a method in the game PHP to perform some server-side action e.g. go to the next player.  endGame is a built-in state which obviously ends the game.  Just need to codify the game end criteria of course.  
    
Per recommendations from the documentation, I built out a document of how I imagined the Gizmos state machine which I am linking here: [https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1piXUGzm7CGsGKuX72Qy2bGQhADzkiuZ8Hu5TssxGrCM/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1piXUGzm7CGsGKuX72Qy2bGQhADzkiuZ8Hu5TssxGrCM/edit?usp=sharing) 

I built it in Google Slides because I like the feature where you can create text boxes, drag arrows from one box to another, and the arrows are anchored such that moving a box moves the arrow with it.  I was actually pretty close with the state machine with the following exception: the intermediate "game" states were unnecessary and even a bit detrimental.  When a user action like buying a card results in a service-layer call (PHP via ajax), if that service throws an error, then the user action is simply rolled back.  Thus it makes much more sense for the entire buying of the card to occur within one state such that an error will leave the user in the same state with the ability to make a different decision.  With an intermediate state, there then needs to be extra code to both handle the error and explicitly push the game back to the appropriate state.  The same idea applies to "sphereRandom".

Other than that I'm quite proud of this initial model.  I won’t get into the implementation details until later, but the high level flow is:

1. Player starts their turn (playerTurn)  
2. Can select a card (or deck to research) or an energy  
   1. If energy, service-layer call to move energy from row to player  
   2. If card, go to cardSelected  
      1. This is another interesting decision.  In theory selecting a card could be a purely client-side action.  I.e. the selected card is not saved to the database in any way and refreshing the page would "undo" the card selection.  I'm still not entirely sure I made the right decision, but at the very least doing it this way (a) makes it easier to show and hide the appropriate action buttons via the JS built-in 'onUpdateActionButtons' and (b) ensures that the player has selected a valid card before letting them try to build or file it.  All in all probably not a hugely important decision but I've thought about it a good deal.  
   3. Following cardSelected, player can cancel (go back to playerTurn and pick something else) or build/file.  Or if they selected a deck research which leads to the same decision.  
3. Crucially following any finalized database changes \- an energy/card being picked/built/filed \- I knew that there would need to be a mechanism to track when a gizmo is triggered. I did not have a clear idea of what that would be at this point so I’ll get to that later.  
4. triggerCheck is a simple routing state: does this player have any unused triggered gizmos?  If yes, go to triggerSelect else go to nextPlayer.  I originally intended for this state to also trigger the appropriate gizmos based on the action performed by the player, but this is much simpler to do from within the dedicated action functions.  
5. Now triggerSelect is the beauty of the state machine.  At this point the user picks one of their built triggered gizmos.  This then goes back to the server to perform the appropriate action based on the effect e.g. draw a random sphere or score a point OR goes to a different player state to do things like pick an energy, research, or buy another card.  As of today I actually haven't gotten to the point of these player actions \- they will likely warrant additional states as I think imposing limits on the existing playerTurn state will be more challenging and less clean.  
   1. Crucially after selecting a trigger and performing its action, the game needs to record that to ensure it cannot be used again the next time the player comes around to triggerSelect  
6. There is then a loop between triggerCheck and triggerSelect until the player has used all of their triggered gizmos or decides to pass without using them all.  Late in the game a player can often trigger 5+ gizmos in a turn.  As build triggers can cascade into pick triggers.  Then nextPlayer and the cycle continues

Okay so the board looks okay and there’s a state machine, but the data is all meaningless \- basically just placeholders.  So how do we make it meaningful?  Well we need to initialize a starting game state and save it into the database.  Enter the BGA backend: MySql/PHP.  The documentation stresses to keep the database light and I don't think any exception needs to be made for Gizmos.  The general principle is to only track information in the database which can actually change in the course of a game.  Any static information about cards such as their cost, color, effect, etc should instead go in your materials php file: a list of associative arrays which may be referenced from game.php.  

I made use of the "Deck" component for tracking the gizmo cards.  My initial intuition was to have 3 separate decks \- one for each level of card.  I started writing out the PHP code to initialize the individual decks and quickly realized that this was nonsensical and would make my life much harder in the long run managing the separate decks.  Instead I settled on a single deck with distinct locations for each level: deck\_1, deck\_2, deck\_3, row\_1, row\_2, row\_3.  Thus the "Deck" utilities for drawing/moving/shuffling cards were still useful as these methods take a "location" parameter for both source and destination if applicable.  The other possible values for location are 'built', 'filed', or 'researched'.  For the former 2, the 'location\_arg' specifies the ID of the player who built or filed that card.  I added a couple other columns to the deck table later which I'll get to in due time.

The energy table was even simpler.  There are 13 energies of each color: blue, black, red, and yellow.  On game start, I instantiate 52 energies in the database.  One minor simplification I made that I'm quite proud of is rather than track the color of each energy, I simply infer it via colors\[ energy.id % 4 \] where colors is an array of the 4 colors.  Thus the energies table is only 2 columns: ID and location where location is one of dispenser, row, or a player's ID.  So game setup defaults the location of all energies to the dispenser and then picks 6 at random for the row.

That covers the database.  Game setup is done in x.game.php and is fairly straightforward: create instances of all game elements, in my case cards and energies, within the database.  This was easy for energies, but to create cards, I need to define what those cards are.  Each of the 3 gizmos decks consist of 36 unique cards.  I did not yet have any game assets, but I was able to find a cheatsheet someone else created and posted online listing all the card effects (I don't actually own Gizmos, have only played it with my brother and friends who do).  I created descriptions of a few cards from each level in my materials to test with.  It's always possible that I'll learn something new which drastically shifts how I am describing the cards so I didn't want to go overboard.

With that I launched a new game, naturally hit a few bugs, but worked my way through them successfully initializing the cards in the database.  Then with the help of the Card component, just a few lines of code randomly drew the 2, 3, and 4 starting cards for the level 3, 2, and 1 rows respectively.  Now refreshing a game loads the same energies and cards each time.  Good progress.

Taking a step back again, for security reasons the client-side JS has no direct access to the database.  It is all facilitated through game.php.  There's a dedicated function in the php to retrieve and set data to be passed to the JS based on which player opened up the game.  In most games it is crucial to pass only the information available to the current player, but Gizmos has no private information which makes this very easy \- pass the energy row, each card row, and all of the built/filed cards.  Then the JS has a dedicated setup function called on page load which parses this information and creates the relevant HTML elements for user interaction.  I’ve spent a lot of time updating exactly how the elements are getting created knowing that needs to look good on mobile, desktop, and others inbetween.  My knowledge is by far the weakest around styling UIs \- I do a lot more service-layer development in my job and when I do touch the UI it’s heavily abstracted.

Next up: basic player actions in picking energies or cards.

## **11 July 2022**

As I touched on briefly earlier, when a user does something in the UI some information has to be passed to the server to make the appropriate database updates.  This is done using ajax.  Naturally whatever action is performed doesn’t always immediately result in submitting the data to the server.  In some cases some validation may be helpful or the data being submitted needs to be collected and/or formatted.  E.g. for picking an energy before making the ajax call a check occurs to ensure the player is not already at their energy capacity.  This isn’t entirely necessary, but calls to the server are slower than client-side.  However the validation on the server *is* entirely necessary.  Anything that *can* be submitted to the server via JS could be *“hacked”*.  There’s nothing stopping an ambitious player from opening up their browser dev console and making the ajax request to pick a bonus energy or worse trying to pick an energy that belongs to another player.  So server-side validation of every user action is crucial.

In a similar vein, and this is well-covered in the documentation, but I found it so interesting that I wanted to write more on it, code needs to be written with SQL injection in mind.  The principle behind this is that if you let the client pass any string to the server and then put that string into a SQL query, a savvy user (or a brute force algorithm) can arbitrarily read or update anything in the database.  One way around this is to simply never pass any strings to the database.  This is what I went with for Gizmos.  All data passed from client to server is either integers or lists of integers where those integers correspond to the unique IDs of gizmo cards and/or energies.  Thus the only validation I need to do server-side is to confirm that the location of the IDs passed are as expected.  If a user is building a gizmo, then it better either be in the row, research, or their archive.  Else throw an error.  If a user is picking an energy, then it better be in the row.  Else throw an error.  Pretty straightforward but crucial to get right or you end up with a game that can be cheated or in other coding situations leaked user data.

The flow for BGA is there’s one file, action php, which stores the names of the allowed server calls.  This file is responsible for parsing out the arguments and then calling another method in the game PHP which then does the actual meat of the method in validating the data and updating the database.  Finally the result of the method is returned to the client via a notification.  The JS file has hooks to listen for specific notifications and then respond accordingly.  Example for picking an energy:  

1. It is playerTurn and player clicks on an energy  
2. JS confirms user is not at energy limit then makes ajax request to server with unique ID of the energy  
3. Server:   
   1. Confirms that energy was in the row  
   2. Updates the database relocating the energy to the active player  
   3. Randomly puts a new energy from the dispenser into the row  
   4. Transition the game state to triggerCheck    
   5. Notifies the JS which also results in a game log entry  
   6. JS then does the appropriate UI updates \- in this case:   
      1. Delete the picked energy element from the row  
      2. Increment the player’s energy count  
      3. Add the new energy to the row

Similar logic applies to building/filing cards except obviously the validation is a bit different.  For filing, need to ensure player is not at archive capacity.  For building, need to also ensure that the player has enough energies of the appropriate color.  But wait, converters.  I skipped over that and called it a problem for another day when more of the game was built out.  So for now simply confirm that player has at least ‘cost’ many energies of the card color.  The energies being spent are passed in an integer array to the server.  The server confirms each of these energies belongs to the player, confirms the card was in an expected location, moves the energies back to the dispenser, moves the card to location=built and location\_arg=player\_id, draws a new card from the appropriate level deck for the row, and notifies the UI to update the player energy count, reposition the card from the row to the player, and move the new card to the row.  Filing is a simplified version of that.

In the iteration of the game at this point, after picking, building, or filing, the state would always transition to the next player as this is how I stubbed out stTriggerCheck (prepending ‘st’ is a convention for identifying that the method is triggered by a game state).  But triggers are the most fun part of Gizmos so I picked that as the next thing to build out.

For a while I considered creating a separate DB table to track triggers with columns for turn number, which card triggered, etc, but this is way overkill.  I quickly arrived at what I still consider to be a better approach.  I added two boolean columns to the card database for “is\_triggered” and “is\_used”.  At the end of every player’s turn (during nextPlayer), these values are reset to false.  Then within the server methods for any action that can possibly trigger a gizmo, the code retrieves the built gizmos for that player and checks if they are triggered by what just happened.  It actually ends up being a bit smarter than that because during the game setup, the card “effect\_type” from the materials static data is put into the card\_type column of the database.  For reference, I named each trigger effect differently: trigger\_pick, trigger\_build, trigger\_file, and then a few more edge cases for level 3 cards like trigger\_buildfromfile and trigger\_buildlevel2.  The part after the underscore is what action triggers the card.  So the trigger check query also filters on effect\_type and further cuts back on the necessary lines of code.  The only logic that occurs post-query is color checks since this is in the static data.  Overview using trigger\_pick as an example:

1. User has picked an energy  
2. PHP method that responds to picking an energy calls a checkPickTriggers method  
3. Query DB for gizmos in location=build, location\_arg=active\_player\_id, card\_type=trigger\_pick  
4. If any are returned, iterate through them:  
   1. Retrieve static data for the gizmo card including the color of energy that it triggers on  
   2. If color of picked energy \== trigger\_color  
      1. Add to list of gizmos to trigger  
5. Update list of gizmos setting is\_triggered \= true

Building a card is very similar except that we first confirm exactly which trigger types should be returned.

1. Define trigger\_types array with trigger\_build in it  
2. If card that was built was from archive  
   1. Add trigger\_buildfromfile to trigger\_types  
3. If card that was built is level 2  
   1. Add trigger\_buildlevel2 to trigger\_types  
4. Query gizmos build by this player where card\_type in trigger\_types  
5. Perform the same checks for color IF card\_type \== trigger\_build (the other two never depend on color)

The logic also needs to account for multiple colors as some trigger gizmos allow for multiple cards.  The static materials simply stores trigger\_color as an array of string colors.  Similarly it also needs to account for the 4 multi-color level 3 gizmos.  So it checks if color matches or if built gizmo is color=’any’ \- an edge case that needs to be accounted for in various places.

So with that in place, the update to stCheckTrigger are very simple: query the database for gizmos built by this player where is\_triggered=1 and is\_used=0.  If there are any: transition to triggerSelect else nextPlayer.  Boom, easy.  Next up: implement triggerSelect.

One crucial thing that I learned while implementing triggerSelect is that all of the handy methods that the Deck component offers only return the columns built into deck and as far as I can tell there is no way to have it return additional columns.  A minor convenience, but that means in order to get my new is\_triggered and is\_used columns to the UI, I have to craft SQL statements instead of a quick call to “getCardsInLocation(‘built’)”.  Oh well.

The rest is fairly simple \- assign a new class to the triggered cards to give them a special border (currently on green, but could change) during trigger select making it obvious to the player that they’ve triggered.  Then once used, assign a different class to make them appear used \- currently a grey border, but it would be cool if I could neutralize the entire card art to greyscale.

When a triggered card is selected during triggerSelect, card id is submitted via ajax, the card details are retrieved including a field called ‘trigger\_action’ which specifies what the card actually does when triggered e.g. draw, pick, score, or a few other level 3 edge cases to be implemented.  Time for another quick digression.

There’s another crucial piece of data to track at this point that the more astute readers may have identified: which card is currently triggering?  Unless the entire trigger from selection to completion is performed client-side, which in my model it is not, this has to be saved into the database in some fashion so we know which card to mark as used when the user has completed their trigger action.  I could add another column to the cards database for “is\_triggering” or something similar, but that’s a bit clunky.  Well the BGA framework offers another handy tool in GameStateVars.  It’s an built-in table that store name integer key-value pairs with low effort implementation at the top of your game PHP.  So I created one of these called “triggering\_gizmo\_id” which works great.  When hitting triggerCheck, if triggering\_gizmo\_id \> 0, set that is\_used=true in the DB and then set it back to 0\.  I also use this feature for “selected\_card\_id” and later “research\_level”.

At this point I could now play a several turns of gizmos including picking spheres, filing gizmos, buying them, and then triggering the purchased gizmos.  I probably haven’t stressed enough thus far how much of my time went toward dynamically building out the UI based on what’s returned in the current game data.  I feel very strongly about using the actual gizmos game board which consists of 5 columns for sorting your gizmos by effect type along with a summary of what actions can be taken on each turn. 

![][image1]

This potentially poses an issue as it can’t really collapse in any fashion on mobile meaning it can only shrink.  Will cards still be readable when 5 of them need to fit side-by-side across a mobile screen?  I honestly don’t know the answer yet \- I sure hope so (spoiler: while writing this I took some screenshots in a very narrow browser so you will find out soon).

The other obvious question is what do I do with the archive?  For a while I was putting it above the board, but that adds a lot more vertical real estate.  I also know for sure that the gizmos for *every* player needs to be visible at all times as other players’ built cards is crucial information for planning what to do on your turn (should I pick/archive this card or pick this one color energy in the row because I know it blocks what my opponent wants to do?).  It’s expected that mobile users will have to scroll sometimes to see the entire gamestate, but I’d still like to keep it to a minimum.

Well a little over a week ago I finally got the game assets including that nice looking board and most importantly all 108 card images.  The first thing I did was google a [spritesheet packer](https://www.codeandweb.com/free-sprite-sheet-packer).  I found one easily and picked it arbitrarily \- did what I needed it to do.  But the other thing I knew I wanted to do based on some early reading was to make my spritesheet style references by background-size percent rather than pixels which as far as I could tell *none* of the free online tools would do.  This is a relatively recent CSS feature which comes with one extremely important benefit: the sprite images can be scaled.  If sprite positions are specified by pixels, then you come away with a statically sized image and scaling is impossible.  If they’re specified by percentage coverage of the spritesheet, then you can specify any height/width on each sprite.  I knew this would come in handy and it most definitely has.  The only drawback is that some older browsers do not yet support it, but I really just don’t care.  Use a chromium browser.  Let IE die already.

Writing out the percentage-based CSS for 108 cards in a spritesheet by hand was not something I wanted to do so I wrote a quick PHP script which probably took a similar amount of time but was much more interesting and will likely come in handy when something inevitably changes.  LIke currently my sprite sheet is all vertical (1x108), but doing a compact spreadsheet where the height and width are closer I believe yields a significantly smaller file.  Also the card images are extremely high res so I ran it through a free online compression tool which roughly halved the size with no noticeable drop in quality.

The other thing about having all the cards is I now felt more confident writing up all the static data describing the cards.  Following a piece of advice from the BGA studio docs, I did this in a spreadsheet: [https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1ssu0Qy6yNqjF1q1nAR8rddf1Xd83Ejxx7gC0kAL3uAw/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1ssu0Qy6yNqjF1q1nAR8rddf1Xd83Ejxx7gC0kAL3uAw/edit?usp=sharing)

The benefits of this are that it is much easier to edit than a PHP associative array and there exist some tools for converting a CSV to PHP.  However I wasn’t happy with any of the tools I found so I wrote a PHP script instead.  It wasn’t very hard \- there are already built in methods for parsing a CSV file into an associative array and now I had the freedom to make all sorts of minor tweaks while loading in the data like refactoring a comma list into an array and dynamically building out tooltips based on card effects.  Definitely a great decision long-term.

Next up is to update the UI to use the sprites.  The only challenge I ran into initially is I forgot to set a pixel width on the cards so they weren’t showing up.  Easy fix, just had to realize what was happening.  I then spent I think close to 10 hours experimenting with various responsive designs for the game board.  I am pretty happy with where I’ve ended up.  The fact that the card size is scalable is integral to the whole idea.  Make the game board take up 100% width of the screen and then specify percentage width slots for each card by row.  Level 1 has 4 cards and the deck, 5 equal width slots.  Each slot needs a bit of spacing between them say 1% margin which means the width of each card should be 18% (1% on each side)\*5 \= 100\.  Then for level 2 to match the gizmos instructions (and to just look plain snazzy), the 4 cards should be centered above level 1\.  Simple, set 10% margins on the entire row container div.  Then within the row, 23% with 1% margins.  Same idea for level 3, 20% margins on the container, 31.333% on the cards, 1% margins.  Everything float left and the result is a true to design gizmos board that looks great on both mobile and desktop.

I also moved the Energy row below the gizmo rows \- it was previously above, but I like this look better so you can see the available energies while also seeing your built cards.  In playing a few rounds I did notice that it’s very clunky to have to scroll up to check your energy counts so I’ll probably need to add that into the player card somewhere as well.

The next challenge was the game boards.  I had already sorted cards by column into the player cards, but as I said before, I knew vertical space was still a concern.  I tinkered a bit with a component called “Zone” and it sort of worked, but I wasn’t sure it would work with my new scalable responsive design because a prerequisite was to set static heights on each zone.  The result was brilliant though as it would then stack all of the elements, in my case cards, within that zone.  Once a gizmo is built all you need to see is the top 25% of the card so this was really elegant.

Well it took me a bit to get there, but the solution was to dynamically calculate the height based on the page width on loading the UI.  I.e. the width of the screen would be the entire div.  The page consists of 5.5 cards across so with margins, card width \= 0.155\*div width and height=width because the cards are squares.  Then while building out each column within the player card, I could calculate the height based off of a formula:  
*1\*height \+ 0.25(num\_cards-1)\*height*   
which simplifies to:  
	*height\*(0.75 \+ 0.25num\_cards)*  
This worked excellently and the cards now stack nicely for each player maximizing vertical screen space.  And because of the dynamic calculations based on screen width it still loads well for any platform.

The next conundrum which I still had not yet tackled was where to put filed cards.  Well as you can already see in the above image, I ended up putting them under the empty Research column and could position them as 50% the width of the built cards.  I still have another problem to tackle there in that stacking the archived cards (once a player has upgraded) is not tenable as it hides the cost in the bottom left corner which is irrelevant for built gizmos, but obviously not filed.  My current thought is to raise the z-index of the selected card so the cost becomes visible once you select it.  Or maybe I assume that if you’ve gotten to the point where you’ve archived multiple cards, you’ve probably built enough others that the archive is no longer the determining factor in the height of your gizmos so they could all be shown in their full height.  Not sure, we’ll see.  Notably hovering over a gizmo does show the full size version of it, but hover of course doesn’t work on mobile so there needs to be a good way for mobile users to check the cost of their filed gizmos.

Well I skipped over a few crucial things to jump to getting the cards working in the UI as it was a pretty rewarding journey and fresh in my mind. So next time I get to this (let’s be real, probably tomorrow because I’m loving this), I’ll talk about upgrades, which are rather boring, research, which is a little more interesting but probably needs more work, and finally converters, which are by far the most interesting of the three and *definitely* needs more work.

## **12 July 2022**

Upgrades first.  All of the non-level 3 upgrades increase capacities for a few player characteristics: energy limit (defaults to 5), archive limit (defaults to 1), and research quantity (defaults to 3).  These capacities are all calculated at the start of a player’s turn and passed to the JS for client-side validation and indicators about what actions will do.  E.g. if a user has selected a deck the button will say Research X so they know how many they’re researching.  Prior to calculating each value, we check the built gizmos for the active player and see if they’ve built any upgrades that contribute to these values.  Very simple.  I have yet to implement the level 3 edge cases that do things like preventing you from filing or researching or score additional points at the end of the game.  They won’t necessarily be difficult, just an edge case that’s not crucial to getting the game to a usable state so low priority.

Researching is also fairly straightforward.  When user has selected a deck and hit the research button, ajax call to server, check the GameStateVar for active\_player\_research\_quantity and selected\_card\_id which in this case is the level deck 1, 2, or 3, move that active\_player\_research\_quantity cards from deck\_X to research, update the game state to research, and send a notification back to the JS to indicate success.

One other useful nugget is that game states accept an “args” function.  This hooks into a function in the game PHP that returns arguments for use in the JS file by the onEnteringState function which is also called on pageload.  So instead of doing a bunch of game state checks on the global setup function, the research cards are returned by this dedicated args function which only triggers when it’s actually needed in the research state.  This researched card data then needs to be parsed and added as elements in the UI.  Thus the next question is where to show these cards?  For now I’m using a popup.  I’m not confident that it will stay this way but it seems to work pretty well.  If state \== ‘research’ create a pop-up, put all the cards in it and show it to all users (researched cards are public knowledge just like everything else in gizmos \- at least I think it is…).  User is then free to select a card just like they would from a row and then build or file it.  They can freely close the pop-up to examine the rest of the game state and then reopen it via a command in the action bar.  This was working prior to implementing the actual card assets so I spent a bit of time getting the sizing right for the cards within the pop-up.  For now I settled on making it the same width as the level 2 row and then dynamically setting the % width of each card based on the number researched so they stretch nicely for the full page width.  Might need to make it go to multiple rows to handle edge cases on mobile in which users have several upgrades and a super high research count.

Moving on to converters which is an ongoing thought experiment as I still have some crucial edge cases to handle.  My thought process was that while in cardSelected, make the player’s built converters clickable as this aligns with the experience in triggerSelect and intuitively makes sense. I’m activating this converter to convert my energy to a new color.  But it also seems important to give clear feedback on the result of these conversions and to validate that it’s a meaningful conversion.  I’ll start with some simple examples then move to some important edge cases.

1. I selected a level 1 red gizmo that costs 1  
   1. Initially the action bar says “Build (1 red)”  
   2. I have no red energy, but I do have a blue energy and a blue to any converter  
   3. I click my blue to any converter and the action label now reads “Build (1 blue)”  
   4. Simple. Elegant  
2. I selected a level 2 blue gizmo that costs 2  
   1. Action bar reads “Build (2 blue)”  
   2. I have one blue energy and a blue to two converter  
   3. I click the converter and the action bar now reads “Build (1 blue)”

Now I’ve selected the same level 2 blue gizmo but all I have is a red to two converter.  If I click on this converter, what should happen?  My initial thought was to show a friendly error like “Cannot convert red energy to build blue gizmo”.  But what if I also have two red to any converters?  Should the user *have* to select those converters before selecting the red to two?  My thought is definitely no.  Intuitively users will expect to convert in the *opposite* direction from the code.  I.e. I have one red energy.  I first want to convert it to two (which the code sees as useless) and then convert them each to blue energy with my other two converters.  The code wants to work backwards from the cost.  Somehow the code needs  to see that this conversion actually *is* useful because of the other two converters.  So that’s exactly what I did.

1. User selects a \<color\> to two converter but \<color\> \!= selected\_gizmo.color  
2. Iterate through user’s built converters, if two \<color\> to any converters are found, automatically select them too  
3. Thus the action bar changes from “Build (2 blue)” to “Build (1 red)” and all the user has done is click on their red to two converter.  The rest is implied.

But now what happens if a user clicks on an already active converter?  Intuitively they should be toggleable such that you can examine your different conversion options e.g. if I have both black and red energies and black and red converters, I may need to spend some time deciding which energy I value less when building a blue or yellow card.  However if I deselect one of those red to any converters, I’m effectively left with 1 red and 1 blue energy which obviously does not work to build my gizmo.  Enter dependencies.  In the logic that automatically selects the additional, there’s a JSON object being built out describing what converters are being used and what the end result is in energy expenditure which is useful both for building the action bar label and for deselecting converters.  So I add an attribute to this object on the selected converters for “supported\_converter” and put this on each of the red to any converters pointing at the ID for the red to two.  Thus when a converter with “supported\_converter” is deselected, the supported converter is also automatically deselected.  Continuing from the above case:

1. Action bar reads “Build (1 red)” and 3 converters are all selected (when selected the gizmos are selected they have a new class assigned which results in a colored border)  
2. User clicks on one of the two any to red converters that are selected  
3. Logic described above deselects the red to any which momentarily updates the energy spend count to 1 blue  
   1. This is because the JSON object tracks energy replacement.  When a red to any converter is selected, that means the player gets to spend one red energy instead of whatever the card costs, in this case blue.  Working in reverse, deselection forces the player to spend a blue energy again instead of red   
4. It then sees that there exists a supported\_gizmo and automatically deselects that too which puts back in a red energy  
5. Result: action bar reads “Build (1 red, 1 blue)” which accurately builds into the gizmo

After doing this, I racked my brain to figure out if I’m missing something.  Will this really work?  It seems too hacky.  I’ve come to the conclusion that it really will work for all the *basic* converters i.e. those that convert from a single color to any color or two energies of that color.  However as with literally everything else I’ve implemented, there are level 3 edge cases and for that matter also level 2 edge cases.  What do I do when a gizmo performs two conversions?  Are there now *three* toggleable states?  I.e. use 1, use 2, not used?  It’s important to allow all 3 in case someone wants to spend 1 red, 1 blue instead of both reds when they have a convert up to 2 red to any converter (a level 2 gizmo).  And then how do I handle the mother of all converters: any to any (dreaded level 3 edge case)?

Everything I’ve done so far has been to avoid the situation where I need to build out a complicated UI where users can freely select from their energy pool, freely select converters, and then “check” what they’ve done, but inevitably, this may need to be the route I go down… I’m not even sure why I’m so hesitant, it just sounds like a lot of work.  My brain jumps to Splendor, a game I’ve played a lot.  In Splendor there are 5 colors of gems and then a “wild” gem.  When building a card, players are free to replace any gems with a wild gem.  So if a player has no wilds, then the game automatically selects the gems that you will spend, but if you have even a single wild, you have to manually select the combination of gems to spend.  A wild gem is a perfect analog for an any to any converter, but just like in Splendor, there’s no reason to throw out my automatic energy selection logic.  This might be another case where I’ll do the initial implementation in a pop-up but maybe later I’ll try to make the energy selection look fancier.  Maybe a zone?  Use the energy ring image asset to stack energies?  We’ll see, but this is on my to do list before alpha.

And with that I think I’ve covered everything I’ve done to date.  I’m sure I missed something but I’m not really aiming for perfect accuracy, just a rough recollection focusing on the things that stood out to me as interesting.  From here on out I plan to record these at the end of each coding session while it’s fairly fresh.  Or perhaps even mid-session.  There’s a popular anecdote around programming called “rubber duckying”.  I don’t know if it’s true but the gist of it is that a developer had a rubber ducky on their desk.  Whenever they were stuck with debugging, they’d start explaining what their code is doing and where it’s hitting issues to the rubber ducky until they figure out what’s happening.  The point is that if you start to explicitly lay out a full (and accurate) explanation of what your code *is* doing and also what you *expect* it to be doing, eventually you’ll arrive at the cause of the discrepancy.  In my job I do this all the time with support emails.  Hi dev support, this is what I’ve written, this is what I want it to do, this is what I’ve tried, this is what happens, so then I tried this, oh wait then I could also try this, oh and wow now it’s working \<discard email\>.  Happens all the time.  The point I’m getting to is that among other things, this diary can act as my rubber ducky.  I’d say it already has (see above outline of where I’m headed with converters).  So with that I’ll lay out the things off the top of my head I know still need to be done and I’ll start with one of them next time I sit down to work on this (except I’m not usually sitting because I’m at a standing desk):

1. Exhaustively define logic for handling all of the various level 3 effect edge cases  
   1. Triggers:  
      1. ~~Pick 2~~  
         1. ~~This one is interesting because it needs to be possible for someone to only use one of the picks, but I also need to not mark the gizmo as completely used if only one pick has occurred.  Technically is\_used is an int because mysql doesn’t have bools so maybe I set it to 2 and handle it differently?~~  
      2. Score (and score 2\)  
         1. VP Token count needs to be tracked separately from overall score (gizmo points) for the level 3 gizmos that count your VP score again  
      3. Research  
         1. I think this and all of the other cases below that require some user input are best handled via dedicated game states rather than client-side modifications on existing ones  
      4. Build level 1 for 0  
      5. And others I’m forgetting  
   2. Upgrades \- these are all flags to pass to client-side and then validation server-side to prevent hacking  
      1. Can’t research  
      2. Can’t file  
      3. Build discount?  
      4. Score per energy at end of game  
         1. This and the next are the exception to the above.  These don’t actually *do* anything during the game.  So just need to add logic that counts them into the player score at endGame  
      5. Score per vp at end of game  
   3. Converters \- should be sufficiently covered by above description for energy free select UI \- just another UI to build which I kind of hate  
      1. All the double-dippers  
      2. Any to any  
2. Define endGame condition in nextPlayer and ensure a full round is completed after it’s met (every player gets same number of turns)  
3. Various minor UI improvements such as:  
   1. Add energy count UI closer to player gizmos to prevent extra scrolling \- this should help out with the converter UI too as these can be made clickable during cardSelected  
   2. Update around gizmo boards to make the player’s name a bit more prominent, color names based on player color, bold or add an icon next to active player  
   3. ~~Reorder the boards to reflect turn order, but always put the current (not active) player at the top~~  
   4. ~~Change the  board image for the starting player to the “special” slightly differently colored board~~

   

And that’s all I can think of for now but there’s surely more.  Until next time.

## **14 July**

Felt like not much got done today.  Spent a good deal of time just stepping through and playing the game.  All the early game things are working great so it was pretty fun.  Actual improvements I made:

1. Starting player now has the “special” looking goldish player board  
2. Current player gizmos card is now above the rows which makes it easier to read your energy counts while perusing options  
   1. I also reduced the max-width for the board to 1000px because the gizmos looked really big on my 1920x1080 monitor.  My thought is to then have a breakpoint that puts the energy row vertically in that extra space.  Probably an energy ring too, but less sure on that  
3. Realized that I need to disconnect onclicks from destroyed HTML elements or memory leaks can occur.  Fairly easy fix once I realized that this.connect has 3 params unlike dojo.connect  
4. Squashed some bugs around reassigning onclicks when elements are destroyed and recreated e.g. from file to player board  
5. For next time, first priority is to fix bug in below screenshot where building a blue gizmo triggered my build a yellow/black.  Will update the materials generation script to format colors into an actual array which may be related

![][image2]  
## **17 July 2022**

Had some ideas on how to handle the more complex converters.  Like I said earlier, basically going to mimic what Splendor does, but this throws a few wrenches in the current build:

1. User selects a card  
2. If user has any converters built, show a different message in the action bar like:  
   1. “Optionally select converters to alter energy payment”  
3. If a “simple” converter is selected, use existing logic  
4. Else (any to any or one that allows for more than one conversion):  
   1. Change action bar message: “Select which energies to spend”  
   2. This warrants a new UI for energy selection

But first I did update the materials generation script to put trigger\_colors into an array instead of comma delimited string (default).  Need to test that it’s working now with the trigger checks.

Update: it was not fixed.  After adding some logging realized it was a simple typo with a single equals on a comparison check.  Stupid, haha, but easy fix.

In testing the above I realized that buying a card mucks up the zone UI.  I.e. the cards get too squashed.  Need to recalculate the zone height when a new card is bought.  As part of this, need to keep better track of the currently built cards.  I have a feeling a lot of my game logic is a bit fragile and relies too heavily on refreshing which is not going to fly given a full game needs to be playable without a single refresh.

Will see if more testing uncovers further issues, but for now was able to fix more simply by refreshing the zone/player columns height whenever a card is built.  zone.getItemNumber (count) was helpful to not need to reexamine the gamedatas gizmos each time.  And adding another gizmo automatically uses the new space.

Decided to do some more edge case trigger testing.  Realized that there are some conflicting naming conventions in that ‘trigger\_build’, ‘trigger\_buildfromfile’, and ‘trigger\_buildlevel2’ all need to go into the same column in the UI, but the UI depends on the effect type for placing gizmos.  So I added some code that shortens them all to ‘trigger\_build’, but this same issue is in the DB as well especially since trigger\_buildfro caps out the varchar limit for card\_type.  So the game setup needed updating as well to ensure these are stored as trigger\_build.  The only time that their full effect is used (and needed) is during checkBuildTriggers at which point the code will check the mt\_gizmos effect instead of from the database.  As far as the UI is concerned, they can be regular trigger\_build cards.

So now that’s working, need to actually implement pick\_two.  My thoughts:

1. Should try to use the same methodology for draw\_3  
2. Requirement: need to be able to cancel after only 1 pick  
3. I think it makes the most sense to go straight from the first pick into the second pick without stopping at triggerSelect.  Set a gamestate var to indicate this and check it on canceling.  
   1. If this is a second use, show a confirmDialog that player wants to lose their second pick  
      1. Confirm dialog may be overkill.  I think just making it clear in the state description is fine:  
         1. “You may pick a second energy. Cancel to skip”  
   2. For draw3, this warrants a new state with action button for “Draw Second/Third”

It’s late so calling it with only the game-side implementation.  Next time need to ensure the client-side is working for picking two energies and then for draw three.  Chipping away…

## **18 July 2022**

Client-side went smoothly.  I updated the description for the triggerSphereSelect state to be dynamic based on an args.  I.e. if the multiple\_uses flag is \>0, use the description in my outline above else use the normal you may pick a sphere description.  Only major hold-up was I needed to return from the switch in st\_triggerAction to ensure I didn’t transition the state to triggerSelect after putting it to triggerSphereSelect again in the case of a pick\_two action.  Did not use a confirm dialog and am confident that’s the right route.

In testing, I realized that I went over my energy capacity.  Both triggerSphereSelect and draw/randomSphere were not ensuring that user was not at capacity.  Easy server-side check that needed to be implemented for any action that results in increasing player energies.

Next up: draw\_3.  New state for triggerDraw with two actions: Draw or Pass.  Dynamic description for “Draw a \<second/third\> energy or cancel to skip”.  Draw submits to triggerSphereRandom which then goes back to triggerCheck.  triggerCheck looks at triggering\_uses: if \<2, increment and loop back to triggerDraw, else do normal behavior.  If player cancels out of triggerDraw, check uses.  If \>0, set gizmo to used, and go to triggerCheck (I did not do this, need to fix), else go back to triggerSelect normally \- this is a bug fix for pick\_2 as well.

Need to go make dinner but will hopefully finish this up later.  Running into issues testing because I think editing the game states can muck up active games.  Will create a new one.

Coming back to this, it was not a problem with the states.  I was editing an old states file from my local PC instead of the one in the cloud, oops.  Oh well started a fresh game anyway and took it as an opportunity to regenerate my materials from CSV with some improvements and clean-up.  Getting draw\_3 to work was about as easy as expected which is great.  Another edge case down.  Eventually I’m going to have to work on converters again.

One more thing I decided to tackle before bed: game progression calculation.  Fairly simple logic which I hope to reuse when doing the endgame logic: query all built gizmos, count them up by player keeping one count of all built for that player and another for level 3s.  Then determine who has built the most and convert into percentages.  End game criteria is when one player has built 16 total gizmos or 4 level 3s.  So most 100 \* most\_built / 16 or 100 \* most\_3s / 4 \- return whichever is greater and that’s the game progression.

Next time I’ll either start to tackle converters or keep chipping away at other edge cases depending on how frisky I’m feeling.

## **19 July 2022**

No converters today.  Finally got around to adding in the game info. Basically copy pasted a bunch of things from boardgamegeek.

Additionally I’m working on getting the research trigger action to work.  Added a new state to select which level to research which then goes into deck selected and hooks into normal research functionality from there.  Only hang-up is to ensure that if deckSelected is canceled, the state goes back to triggerSelect instead of playerTurn.  Handling this by checking the triggering\_gizmo\_id.  If \>0 and state \== ‘deckSelected’, cancelTrigger else cancel.  A lot of states are sharing the cancel ajax action which is handy.

Hit another bug around clearing the triggering\_gizmo\_id when a researched selected card is canceled.  Worked around it by adding a check to not clear triggering\_gizmo\_id for state \= ‘researchCardSelected’.  Will need to do similar for the “build for free/discount” triggers, but calling it a night for today.

## **25 July 2022**

Finally starting to tackle the converter issue.  I’ve added a static energy ring for the current player to show all the energies they have.  Looks really nice:

![][image3]  
Notably I still need to make it work for mobile.  It’s absolute positioned in the top right and my thought is that I can simply update the “top” position to be equal to the height of the gizmos container making it appear in the empty nook to the upper right of row 3\.  Might need to adjust the dimensions as well, but overall I like it a lot. The elliptical fit on zone is sweet.

As part of this also needed to make a bunch of updates to the sphere spend/add notification functions.  I.e. on pick sphere, if current player, slide to energy ring.  Nothing crazy difficult, but I’m feeling less and less confident about the general organization of my code every day I work on this.  Meh, I’m a scrappy developer \- might come back and clean it up later, but realistically probably not.

Coming back to this a couple hours later, added a breakpoint at 1470px to drop the energy ring below the player gizmos.  Also using left instead of right for larger screens so it’s not way off to the right.  Just like the gizmo column zones, needed to add logic that automatically updates the top offset of the ring when current purchases a gizmo that makes their card bigger.  Eventually should add listeners for when window size changes to recalculate height offset in response.  But by-and-large the most important cases to handle are on page load.

Very happy with how this looks.  Ring is only shown for current player which I think is fine \- you can see other players’ counts in the player cards.  So next step here is to utilize the ring for converters.  To reiterate the thought here:

1. onCardSelect: examine the player’s spheres:  
   1. If enough spheres of cost color exist, slide them from ring to card  
   2. Else build dynamic description:  
      1. If Converters exist “Select converters or file”  
      2. Else hide the build button? \- disable  
2. On selecting a converter:  
   1. If use of converter can be inferred:  
      1. If spheres are on card, slide back to ring  
      2. Slide new valid spheres from ring to card  
   2. Else update description to: select which energy to convert  
      1. Update the sphere onclick to slide them in and update converter usage accordingly

It’s a lot, but I think it should work reasonably well.  Until next time.

## **26 July 2022**

Implement this for mobile button usage: [https://boardgamearena.com/forum/viewtopic.php?f=12\&t=26317](https://boardgamearena.com/forum/viewtopic.php?f=12&t=26317)  
/\* make space between buttons on touch devices \*/  
@media (hover: none) {  
  \#page-title .action-button.bgabutton, \#page-title .bgabutton {  
    margin-bottom: 8px;  
    margin-top: 8px;  
  }  
}

I started setting up the logic for sliding spheres to the selected card.  For some reason animations are not playing well.  Maybe laggy browser or I need to compress images more.

Coming back later \- they seem to work better now, not sure what changed.  I finally took a chance to browse through the Abyss codebase.  Primarily to learn about separating my code into multiple tiles.  It seems to be a great example of this.  Man I have so much to learn.  The developer for Abyss created a single helpers.js file that contains several definitions of what effectively amount to classes.  Each class contains helper functions that apply to utilities around managing that thing in the game.  There’s one for Lord, Ally, Location, etc.  I want to follow a similar model, but I’m hitting some analysis paralysis with moving existing code into this file.  I have loads of references throughout the main js file to vars scoped within “this” context. I guess those vars need to be passed as arguments into the helper functions?  Or pass “this” itself?  Does that defeat the purpose of modularizing?  I assume somewhat but certainly not entirely.

Currently going with the approach of setting the vars within the classes instead of within the game file which seems better.  However still running into loads of scope issues to be addressed.  Most of them seem to boil down to having a “Game” class which tracks some shared vars like selected\_card\_id, activePlayer, and more in the future.

Well, happy to say I’m going to bed with the converters working as-is from the helper class.  Still a lot of work to be done on converting, but happy to have it out of the main js file and more importantly I have a better understanding of how I can start building a cleaner more modular codebase.

## **27 July 2022**

Continuing to work on converters, but didn’t have much time today.  I was fairly set on making the “double converters” (convert X to any twice) work via replacing the buttons in the action bar.  I.e. there needs to be a way for user to choose to only convert 1 instead of both.  I still may go with this approach but can’t decide if it’s too clunky.  Other option could be to add a third toggle to the gizmo i.e. off, click to convert 1, click to convert 2, back to off.  How to make this clear via the UI though?  Show a success notification indicating clicking again will convert another?  Put a border on only half the card?  Draw a box around one of the converts?

Hm… will need to think more on it.  In starting to implement the action button approach, maintaining a revertible state once user has selected 1 or 2 is a huge pain given converters are selectable in at least 3 different states each with unique buttons and description.  Also need to implement a “from\_number” for tracking the active converters.

I’m leaning toward the toggling route at this point, but not sure.

## **30 July 2022**

First order of business today was to scrap the one/two buttons and instead toggle the converters.  I’m liking it a lot more.  The visual indicator I first tried was to do a non-solid border, either dotted or dashed.  But for some reason this is causing issues with sprite positioning.  Well literally in writing that sentence I thought of a better phrasing for my google search: “border-style sprite position css” which yielded a wonderful stack overflow post: [css \- Border Position \- Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33942849/border-position)

How have I literally never heard of the “outline” CSS attribute?  Probably because I do so little web development haha.  Well that fixes all of my problems here.  Out with borders, in with outlines.  This fixes the weird effect that happens when you apply a border to an element where it “shifts” a bit to one side to make room for the border.  Holy crap this makes such a big difference, I love it.

So that was surprisingly easy, I think I’m happy with the double any converters.  Next is any to any.  The idea here (as probably stated numerous times previously) is that after clicking the any-to-any converter, the description in the action bar is updated to “Select an energy to convert”.  User can now select an energy from in their ring and then the code is re-executed as if they had selected a convert from \<that color\> to any.  Seems simple enough.

It was fairly simple except that I stupidly started using “throw” for unexpected cases in JS because I expected the error message to be visible.  Turns out it’s not and BGA throws an incomprehensible error as a result.  I’m sure the docs say this somewhere, but oh well, got through it and replaced throw with showMessage instead.  Any to any seems to work great.

Next steps: ensure that the “tryFindTwo” is compatible with both any2 and any to any.  Should prioritize using any2 \> color to any \> any to any due to flexibility of conversion.  After that need to run some thorough tests of weird combinations of converters.  I still have the feeling that I’m missing something bizarre, but at the same time I think it’s just “loops” of converters which there is no use case for.  I.e. I’m buying a red gizmo.  I converted my blue to red.  Theoretically I could then convert my black to blue, but why would I do that?  I should have only used the black to red converter.  Don’t think there’s any need to support that.

Then the server-side converter handling needs implementing.  Need to prevent “cheating” the card buying by adventurous users AND setting converters to used.

Okay I’ve come up with the counterexample to my previous thoughts.  I’m building a 2-red gizmo.  I have a blue-doubling converter, a yellow to any converter, and two blue to any converters.  I have one yellow energy.  I can convert my yellow to blue, double it, and then use both blue to any converters for two reds.  This is problematic with how I’ve written the code as it assumes you always want to convert your energy to the color you’re building.  So user would click their yellow to any converter, it would go to red, and then they’re stuck when converting to blue actually would have worked.  I need to think on that…

So for that case I have already written logic around when the blue doubler is selected.  The code searches for the two blue to any converters and automatically selects them.  However this does assume that they actually have a blue energy.  It could go one step further and if no blue energy exist, try to find another energy that does exist with a corresponding converter.

Ugh, I’m again coming back to the need to do a color-conversion UI which would basically be a full redesign of the converter system I’ve put a lot of time toward building, but the more I think about it the more I think it’s the right move.  The “forwards” route for conversion is so much more intuitive with how Gizmos is actually played as opposed to working backward from the cost which is what my current code does.

New design:

1. User selects a converter  
2. Does user have the required color to convert?  
   1. No? Error (other converter has to be selected first to produce color)  
   2. Yes? Slide that energy from ring to converter  
3. Pop-up absolute positioned near converters column \- should look something like a color wheel or maybe reuse the energy icon  
   1. This is the part I’m most concerned about  
4. The user clicks the color they want to convert to  
   1. Slide the resulting energy to the card being built  
5. The “Build” button no longer shows the full cost but instead how much of the cost you’ve paid based off what’s selected (X / Y \<color\>)  
6. Build button still gets disabled until full cost is selected

## **1 Aug 2022**

Turns out (3) for the color picker UI is actually very easy.  I crafted an HTML div that contains one energy of each color in a 2-by-2 grid.  Made it look nicer with a bit of transparency, a shadow, rounded edges.  When user clicks on a color converter, this block is inserted into the converter div, top is calculated to align with the card and left is set to the width of the card so it appears right next to it on top of the file triggers.

However as usual there was something I failed to consider.  The color picking and build button updates work great when you’re converting your way to building something, but what if you want to spend your energy as-is?  I’m not sure I like the idea of the user needing to click on each individual from the ring to select it.  What is the expected feedback mechanism?  If the card is in the research dialog, where do I slide it?  Maybe show it’s selected via an outline instead?  Yeah let’s try that.

Not sure on the outlined energies.  Might work.  Needed a break from converting so I finally fixed the card alignment issues.  In the above screenshot you can see that there’s a thin white bar at the bottom of the Upgrade card.  This is due to a discrepancy in the card image assets sent by Gizmos’ publisher.  All of the card images were precisely 490px by 490px *except* for 6 of them that were 490x49**4**.  Why?  I have no clue.  It was the last 6 level 2 cards.  So I put each of them into Gimp, cropped off the bottom 4 pixels, regenerated the spritesheet (this time compact), made some updates to my CSS generation script, and copied it all into the game.  Now the cards look squeaky clean.  It’s incredible how much more polished the game looks with some shadows.

Spent a bit more time prettying the conversion process.  A few additional things I’ve mentally mapped out and will need to implement:

1. If a card costs one, no need for user to select energy conversion colors \- it’s always a one-step process and at most 1 converter can be used \- apply existing code (should be working)  
2. On entering a card selected state, automatically select/outline all the player’s energies of that color in their ring (up to card cost)  
   1. Build button should show the (X / Y \<color\>) indicator.  If X \< Y, button is disabled  
   2. Selected energies should be “deselectable” which then updates the button label  
3. On selecting a converter:  
   1. Energy of appropriate color slides from ring to converter  
      1. If all energies of that color are selected, automatically deselect one  
   2. User picks what they convert that energy to  
   3. Energy slides from picker onto other side of converter  
   4. Assign IDs to these energies like from/to\_\<gizmo\_id\>  
   5. Need to ensure structure for tracking conversion process is sound  
4. If conversion is putting spend gems above the cost of the gizmo, should auto deselect the color gems in the ring

As usual I feel like that should cover it, but I’m sure I’ll run into some unforeseen issues when I continue implementing it.

## **15 Aug 2022**

Continuing with converters.  Handling the minutia around sliding spheres from the ring and color picker to an appropriate position on the converter.  Will need to call a cleanup function on leaving cardSelected states to destroy all spent energies OR return them to their original position depending on if card was purchased, filed, or canceled.  In rereading the outline from previous session, realizing this also needs to occur on deselecting a gizmo.  Should be workable by querying energies within the parent card HTML.

Added convert\_from and convert\_to classes for positioning the energy that’s being converted on the converter and for ease of clean-up after.  Convert\_from will not be selectable, but needs to return to ring if originally from there.  Plan is to make convert\_to selectable in the same way as ring energy such that someone could convert an energy to a different color then select the temp energy from the picker for duplication or whatever else.

Made progress on the level 3 duplicate two different colors, but a bit fuzzy on the full implementation still.  Current process is:

1. Select the card  
2. Description changes to “Pick X or Y energy to duplicate” and card is half\_selected  
3. User selects an energy  
4. Validate appropriate color, apply duplicator  
5. Change description back and card is selected

The problem is that currently the code assumes that selecting the card again should result in deselecting it.  Will need a third selected state.  Maybe “picking” should look different from “half\_selected”?  There’s also the problem of determining what the user actually wants to do \- deselect or convert again.  It may be safe to assume that if it’s *possible* to duplicate again, then they want to at which point a third click will deselect entirely.  They can then start over selection to pick the other color as needed.  I think that will work.

Gosh this is turning out to be a bit of a pain.  Just gotta keep chippin’ away…

## **16 Aug 2022**

I desperately need to simplify/streamline the conversion logic.  Running into cases with the tryFindTwoColor that result in incrementing the X / Y color indefinitely.  This is by far the most complex part of this game at least from a coding perspective.  So many assumptions I want to make but can’t due to fringe edge cases.

Major overhaul of the converter implementation: I was still using my naive first approach with a spend\_spheres object consisting of a count of each energy color the player has to pay.  So it starts at { \<card.color\>: \<card.cost\> } and is updated as user selects converters.  This inherently suggests working backwards from the cards cost which I’m moving away from.  Instead I now have an array of selected\_spheres and an object for spending\_power.  Selected is the IDs of spheres that the player will pay and spending\_power tracks what the selected spheres can buy in tandem with selected converters.  So far I think this is much cleaner, but I’m questioning myself a lot given how optimistic I’ve been in the past about some of my design decisions.

Feeling even better about it with more testing.  I can now uselessly convert from color to color as any good Gizmos player should.

Next steps: when selecting a gizmo that can only take one color, prioritize using convert\_tos rather than from the ring.  Maybe give these a little bit of greyscale so they’re more easily differentiated.  Also would be great if I could get the animations working \- might need to get callbacks working like Abyss.  In any case, great progress today.

## **4 Sept 2022**

More converter work.  Fixed an inconsistency where you have to explicitly select an energy from the ring before being allowed to convert it \- now auto-select occurs if spend\_power \= 0 for that color.

Next need to work out handling the temp energy created by duplicating an energy.  I am trying to make the conversion consistent where if activating a converter could allow for multiple selections, the user must explicitly select which energy that is.  This first made me think that I need to place temp energy on the duplicator, but this makes the UI over that card pretty busy.  So instead, the user can select the already converted energy which creates the temp energy image and slides it to the selected converter.  I.e. the temp energy is not shown until it is used for something.

Implementing this:

1. User is in state where they are selecting which energy to spend  
2. Clicks a convert\_from  
3. Confirm that the energy is on a duplicator  
4. Confirm that energy from duplicator has been “used” less than twice  
5. Pass selected energy into toggleConverter  
6. New logic to recognize this as a convert\_from, create the temp energy, and put it on the converter

The happy path for this is working when using an any2 doubler and then a any to any.  Need to apply this same logic toward using a \<color\> to any such that it prioritizes using a loose duplicated energy over auto-selecting real energy in the ring.

I also want to make the distinction between temp energy and actual energy source from the ring more obvious.  First pass at this is a bit of opacity.  Will see how that pans out.

## **11 Sept 2022**

Apparently BGA auto-closes games if a move hasn’t taken place for too long so my nice testing setup is gone.  That’s okay not too hard to recreate and now I’m forced to retest a bunch fo game things I haven’t looked at for a while during the early game / setup.  So brief detour with some random bug fixes…

1. JS error as a result of energy count spheres being uninitialized.  Fix: refactored sphere\_count init into a function that sets spheres to an empty array for each player  
2. Filing a card sends it to the wrong column.  Fix: notif\_cardBuiltOrFiled now correctly recognizes if the player chose built vs filed and puts the card in the appropriate zone  
3. Selecting a card as active player caused a JS error for other players.  Fix: only adjust Build button CSS for active player where it actually exists  
4. Build button disabled for a level 1 gizmo with one energy despite having that energy.  Fix: need to refresh the build button state on selecting energy  
5. Triggerable card yields error when trying to click to trigger.  Fix: selectable condition was taking precedence \- moved triggerable priority above selectable.  
6. Picked and drawn spheres did not respond when clicked on in the ring.  Fix: need to reassign the onEnergySelect function for these spheres  
7. Selecting a deck yields a JS error.  Fix: ensure selected card is not a deck before trying to check gizmo details.  
8. Others…  
9. Converting an energy to a different color and then trying to duplicate it.  Fix: what I was originally going to work on today\!  The temp energy from converters needs to be “visible” to the game logic such that it can be used and moved for other converters.  
   1. When temp energy is created, push it into a different array  
   2. Check this array first whenever auto-selecting energy for converters  
   3. After implementing, this works but naturally more is needed  
   4. If selected converter used a temp energy, add supported to the converter that produced it  
   5. AND if converter is deselected instead of just deleting temp energy, first check if the converter from which that energy originated is still selected and move it back if so  
      1. I think this will fail in testing with chains \>2 of converting a temp energy because it will always go back to the source  
      2. Solution: save this as an attribute on the converter obj  
         1. I have applied this but it still very much needs more testing  
10. Added logic to update player score after a gizmo is built  
11. Added notification to update victory point count after victory point gained

And once again I feel I am back to converters.  Current conundrums:

1. Use a double converter \- should this generate temp energy on the card?  For consistency I feel that maybe it should, but it does kind of bog down the UI  
   1. If I don’t, how is it clear how many energies remain on a doubler for conversion/spending?  
   2. Maybe place a number?  
2. Auto-selection: I could completely remove this which *might* make my life easier overall.  I.e. when a converter is selected, user is always prompted to select which energy to convert.  
   1. The alternative is to make the logic smarter as far as selecting free temp energy before energy from the ring.

Right now leaning toward placing temps for (1) and removing auto-selection for (2).  But done for tonight.  Feels like I’m getting so close, but the converters are a huge can of worms.

## **12 Sept 2022**

Working on getting the logic around temp energies working today.  Specifically placement of multiple energies on each side.  The double color converters can have 2 froms and 2 tos(should fit nicely in one row).  The double double converters can have 2 froms and 4 tos \- I think the tos will have to go in 2 rows.

Okay but I came here to work my way through an issue:

1. First temp energy is just a convert\_to  
2. Each additional temp energy must have a class applied to adjust the positioning  
   1. Maybe t2, t3, t4?  
      1. T pulls double duty as “to” and “temp” (all tos are temps)  
   2. And f2 for from  
3. This ended up being not that complicated

This seems to work nicely.  And I have kept the auto-selection.  I think it’s reasonable to automatically convert temp energies if any exist and it’s easy to back out of a conversion to first convert to a temp energy as needed.

Next: ensure that the double double converters are working and look reasonable.  May need to tweak the styling as tokens are already running over onto the next converter making it a bit hard to click the right one (even harder on mobile).  Smaller tokens or less padding on the top or greater distance between gizmos in the stack.  Plenty of options.
